Recession Model — Data Exploration Report

Generated 2026-05-03 · Data source: recession.db · Read-only inspection · Spec v1.1.0 review

Purpose. Step (b) sanity-checks the data we ingested in Steps 2–2.6 before we build modeling infrastructure on top of it. Confirms that the spec's theoretical assumptions about each feature actually show up in the historical data, and informs whether the v1.1 spec additions (13 new features, T5 market stress target, M5 coincident regime model) are justified by current evidence.

1. Sanity alerts (most important)

  • ℹ️ T1 (NBER): 95 recession months out of 796 (11.9% base rate)
  • ℹ️ T2 (drawdown): 88 months in drawdown out of 797 (11.0%) — history 1960-01-01 → 2026-05-01 (SP500 from Yahoo via committed CSV)

2. Coverage map

Which features have data in which years. Sparse rows = candidates for sub-sample analysis or feature drop.

3. Univariate predictiveness vs NBER (h=12)

Bivariate AUC of featuret predicting USRECt+12. No model fitting — just rank-based separability via Mann-Whitney U.

Reading guide: AUC > 0.65 = strong univariate signal; 0.55–0.65 = useful in a model; below 0.55 = mostly noise on its own.

3b. Univariate predictiveness vs T2 market drawdown (h=6)

Bivariate AUC of featuret predicting T2t+6 (SP500 ≥15% drawdown 6 months ahead). Different horizon than Section 3: market drawdowns develop faster than NBER recessions.

This is critical for v1.1's T5 (Market Stress) target. Features that score high here but low in Section 3 are market-stress predictors, not recession predictors — which is exactly the separation T5 is designed to surface.

3c. T1 vs T2 disagreement

Features ranked by |AUC(T1) − AUC(T2)|. Large positive: predicts recessions but not drawdowns. Large negative: predicts drawdowns but not recessions. These are the candidates that justify having T1 and T5 as separate targets.

4. Pre-recession behavior

For each NBER recession in our sample, looks at the most-recession-favoring value the feature reached in the 12 months before onset (not at onset itself, since signals like the yield curve often peak earlier in the window and rebound). Compares that extremum to the feature's typical level (60-month median ending right before the 12mo window).

  • n_episodes — how many recessions have data this far back
  • n_consistent — how many of those episodes saw the feature visit its recession-favoring zone in the 12mo window
  • mean Δ to recession zone — average distance the extremum moved into the recession-favoring direction (positive = moved toward recession zone). Compare across features cautiously since unit scales differ.
  • direction — implied by univariate AUC (Section 3)
  • consistent? — ✓ if ≥60% of episodes show recession-zone visit, ~ if 40-60%, ✗ if <40%
Featuren_episodesn_consistentmean Δ to recession zonemedian Δdirectionconsistent?

7. Conclusions: Step 4 implications

Implications for Step 4 (feature pipeline):
  • 6 features show strong univariate signal (AUC>0.65): DTWEXBGS, T10Y3M, NFCI, DRTSCILM, INDPRO, REAL_FFR_GAP. These should anchor M1 (static probit). PCA components likely load heavily on them.
  • 5 features show near-noise univariate AUC (0.45–0.55): SAHMREALTIME, CFNAI, ISRATIO, PERMIT, BAA10Y. Consider whether to keep them — may help in a multivariate model, but cost degrees of freedom if they don't.
  • Hamilton (2018) detrending needed for any feature showing a long-term trend (visible in §6 plots) — INDPRO, SP500 levels, possibly DTWEXBGS.
  • PCA reduction will collapse high-correlation pairs flagged in §1 alerts. Likely 3 PCs capture >60% of variance.

8. v1.1 spec implications

Implications for v1.1 spec additions:
  • ✅ T2 (drawdown) now usable. Yahoo SP500 backfill gave T2 88 months of drawdown labels covering 1962, 1969, 1973-74, 1981-82, 1987 (Black Monday, no recession), 1990, 1998 (LTCM, no recession), 2000-02, 2008-09, 2011 (no recession), 2020, 2022. Was 6 events / FRED-only before. Both T2 itself and the new T5 (market stress) target are viable in v1.1.
  • T5 layer is justified by data. 3 feature(s) predict T2 drawdowns substantially better than T1 recessions: BAA10Y, CFNAI, EBP. Without a separate T5 target these would be averaged-out noise in T1 models.
  • 4 feature(s) are recession-specific (predict T1 but not T2): DCOILWTICO, DTWEXBGS, REAL_FFR_GAP, T10Y3M. These should anchor M1/M2 for T1 specifically.
  • ⚠️ Labor expansion questionable for h=12. Existing labor features average AUC=0.57 (T1 h=12) — below the 0.6 threshold. Sahm/CFNAI are coincident, not leading. The 4 new labor features (CCSA, AWHMAN, TEMPHELPS, JTSLDR) should serve M5 (coincident regime model) primarily, not h=12 forecasting.
  • Credit channel: AUC mean = 0.66 across BAA10Y, EBP, NFCI. Strong signal. Adding 2 term-structure variants (T10Y2Y, near-term forward) likely incremental — would test in Step 4.
  • Yield curve baseline: T10Y3M univariate AUC = 0.80. This is the published NY Fed result; M1 baseline should match.
  • Housing channel: PERMIT alone AUC=0.52. v1.1 adds NAHB sentiment + existing home sales. Marginal based on this signal.
  • Inflation features absent from current data. v1.1 adds Core CPI, Core PCE, wage growth (3 features). Cannot validate from this exploration; recommend adding and testing in Step 4.
  • T5 (Market Stress) inputs all present and validated. T10Y3M, BAA10Y, NFCI, and SP500 (now full history) all show meaningful AUC vs T2 drawdowns. Recalibrate the BAA10Y threshold from rolling-90th percentile to absolute (per spec v1.1.1 patch) since the BAA10Y range is too narrow for percentile logic to fire usefully.

End of report. Generated by recession/research/explore.py.
